1: eli eli lamah azavtani (ʾēlî ʾēlî lāmāh ʿăzabtānî): 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' The opening of the Passion Psalm in its Hebrew form. The Aramaic at the cross (Mark 15:34: Eloi eloi lema sabachthani) uses the Aramaic form of the Psalm's Hebrew opening, suggesting Jesus was quoting from memory in the vernacular. The Psalm moves from dereliction (Psalms 22:1-21) to proclamation and praise (Psalms 22:22-31) — ending not in abandonment but in universal witness ('all the ends of the earth shall turn to the LORD', Psalms 22:27). The early church understood Jesus's citation of the Psalm's opening as invoking the whole Psalm, including its vindication ending.
2: ʾĕlōhay ʾezʿaq yômām wĕlōʾ taʿăneh (ʾĕlōhay ʾezʿaq yômām wĕlōʾ taʿăneh): 'O my God, I cry by day, but you do not answer.' The sustained cry yômām ... wĕlāylâ ('by day ... and by night') conveys relentlessness — prayer without intermission yet without response. The phrase wĕlōʾ dûmîyâ lî, 'and I have no silence/rest,' is better read 'there is no silence for me' — the psalmist cannot stop crying out because the distress is too great. The silence of God is the opposite of the heard cry in Psalms 9:12; it is the extremity that makes this psalm the most anguished in the Psalter.
3: wĕʾattâ qādôš yôšēb tĕhillôt yiśrāʾēl (wĕʾattâ qāḏôš yôšēḇ tĕhillôṯ yiśrāʾēl): 'Yet you are holy, enthroned on the praises of Israel.' The waw-adversative wĕʾattâ ('but you') introduces the theological anchor: God's holiness and his enthronement on praise are not negated by the psalmist's experience of abandonment. The noun tĕhillôt ('praises') used as the seat of divine enthronement means that Israel's worship constitutes the medium in which God is present and sovereign — a remarkable theology of liturgy. The verb yōšēb ('sit/dwell') connects to the divine throne imagery of Psalms 9:7 and Psalms 11:4.
4: bĕkā bāṭĕḥû ʾăbōtênû bāṭĕḥû wattĕpallĕṭēm (bĕḵā ḇāṭĕḥû ʾăḇōṯênû bāṭĕḥû wattĕp̄allĕṭēm): 'In you our fathers trusted; they trusted, and you delivered them.' The double bāṭĕḥû ('they trusted ... they trusted') is emphatic — the ancestral trust was complete and unequivocal. The result: wattĕpallĕṭēm ('and you delivered them') — a perfect of completed action. The pattern establishes precedent: trust in YHWH has historically issued in rescue. The psalmist invokes this history as the basis for his own prayer — not to claim guaranteed deliverance but to remind God of his own track record.
5: ʾēlêkā zāʿăqû wĕnimmalāṭû (ʾēlêḵā zāʿăqû wĕnimmalāṭû): 'To you they cried and were saved.' The verb zāʿaq ('cry aloud, cry for help') is repeated from Psalms 22:2 in a contrasting pattern: the fathers cried (Psalms 22:5) and were saved; the psalmist cries (Psalms 22:2) and is not answered. The phrase bĕkā bāṭĕḥû wĕlōʾ bôšû, 'in you they trusted and were not put to shame,' uses bôš ('be ashamed, disappointed') — the ultimate fear of the one whose prayer goes unanswered. The fathers' non-shame is the psalmist's hope and the ground of his continued crying.
6: wĕʾānōkî tôlaʿat wĕlōʾ-ʾîš (wĕʾānōḵî ṯôlaʿaṯ wĕlōʾ-ʾîš): 'But I am a worm and not a man.' The contrast wĕʾānōkî ('but I') is emphatic: the honored ancestors (Psalms 22:4-5) versus the degraded present. The noun tôlaʿat ('worm') is used for a crimson-producing grub (tôlāʿ); to be a worm is to be beneath the dignity of human personhood. The phrase ḥerpat ʾādām ûbĕzûy ʿām, 'a reproach of mankind and despised by the people,' uses ḥerpâ ('reproach, taunt') and bāzâ ('despised') — social contempt that reduces a person to sub-human status. The NT applies this to Christ's passion (cf. Isaiah 53:3).
7: kol-rōʾay yilʿăgû-lî (ḵol-rōʾay yilʿăḡû-lî): 'All who see me mock me.' The verb lāʿag ('to mock, deride') describes the social humiliation of the lamenter. The specific gestures — yaptîrû bĕśāpâ yānîʿû rōʾš, 'they make mouths at me, they wag their heads' — are the recognized gestures of contempt and derision in the ancient world (cf. Job 16:4; Lamentations 2:15). Matthew explicitly cites this verse in the passion narrative: 'those who passed by derided him, wagging their heads' (Matthew 27:39), understanding the mockers at the cross as fulfilling the posture described here.
8: gōl ʾel-YHWH yĕpallĕṭēhû (ḡōl ʾel-YHWH yĕp̄allĕṭēhû): 'He rolled his burden upon the LORD; let him deliver him.' The qal imperative gōl ('roll!') draws on the idiom of rolling a burden onto another (cf. Proverbs 16:3). The mockers' quotation — yitpallĕṭēhû kî ḥāpeṣ bô, 'let him rescue him, for he delights in him' — is savage irony: if YHWH truly favors this person, let him prove it by rescue. Matthew 27:43 cites this verse directly: 'He trusts in God; let God deliver him now, if he desires him; for he said, I am the Son of God.' The verbal correspondence between the Psalm's mockers and the crucifixion mockers is exact.
9: kî-ʾattâ gōḥî mibbāṭen (kî-ʾattâ ḡōḥî mibbāṭen): 'Yet you are he who took me from the womb.' The particle kî ('yet, for') introduces a counter-argument to the mockery: the psalmist appeals to the intimacy of his origins. The verb gōḥî (hiphil of gāḥ, 'to burst forth, draw out') describes the violent creative act of birth — YHWH was the one who drew him out. The phrase maḇṭîḥî ʿal šĕdê ʾimmî, 'you made me trust at my mother's breasts,' means that even the infant's trust in the mother's provision was mediated by YHWH — divine care precedes rational faith.
10: ʿālêkā hošlaktî mērāḥem (ʿālêḵā hošlaktî mērāḥem): 'Upon you was I cast from birth.' The hophal hošlaktî ('I was cast/thrown') — a striking passive that places the infant's placement in God's hands — inverts the language of abandonment: the psalmist was not abandoned but cast upon YHWH from the womb (mērāḥem, 'from the womb'). The phrase mibbeten ʾimmî ʾēlî ʾāttâ, 'from my mother's womb you have been my God,' makes the covenant relationship pre-rational and pre-voluntary — YHWH is 'my God' from before the psalmist could choose it. This grounds the prayer: if you have been my God since birth, you cannot abandon me now.
11: ʾal-tirhaq mimmennî kî ṣārâ qĕrôbâ (ʾal-tirhaq mimmennî kî ṣārâ qĕrôbâ): 'Be not far from me, for trouble is near.' The negative imperative ʾal-tirhaq ('do not be far') is the direct petition — the counterpart to the lament of Psalms 22:1 (God is far). The crisis: ṣārâ qĕrôbâ ('trouble is near') contrasts with YHWH who seems far — nearness and farness are inverted. The phrase kî ʾên ʿôzēr, 'for there is no helper,' identifies the absence of human aid: YHWH is the only recourse, which makes his apparent absence catastrophic.
12: sĕbābûnî pārîm rabbîm ʾabbîrê bāšān kiṯṭĕrûnî (sĕḇāḇûnî p̄ārîm rabbîm ʾabbîrê ḇāšān kiṯṭĕrûnî): 'Many bulls encompass me; strong bulls of Bashan surround me.' The imagery of bulls — pārîm rabbîm ('many bulls') and ʾabbîrê bāšān ('strong bulls of Bashan') — draws on the famous breeding-stock of Bashan's well-fed pastures (cf. Amos 4:1, where Samaria's oppressive wealthy are called 'cows of Bashan'). The encircling movement — sĕbābûnî ... kiṭṭĕrûnî — suggests being surrounded, cut off, with no exit. The enemies are powerful, well-fed, and coordinated.
13: pāṣû ʿālay pîhem ʾaryēh ṭārēp wĕšōʾēg (pāṣû ʿālay pîhem ʾaryēh ṭārēp̄ wĕšōʾēḡ): 'they open wide their mouths at me, like a lion ravening and roaring.' The verb pāṣâ ('to open wide') combined with the lion imagery — ʾaryēh ṭārēp ('a lion tearing prey') wĕšōʾēg ('and roaring') — creates the soundscape of predation. The open mouth of the lion is simultaneously an attack (devouring) and a threat display (roaring). The enemies' open mouths echo the mockers of Psalms 22:7 — hostility expressed through the mouth, whether by derision or by consuming violence.
14: kammayim nišpaktî wĕhitpardĕdû kol-ʿaṣmôtāy (kammayim nišpaktî wĕhiṯp̄ardĕdû ḵol-ʿaṣmôṯāy): 'I am poured out like water, and all my bones are out of joint.' The two images — poured water and disjointed bones — describe the utter dissolution of physical integrity. The pual nišpaktî ('I am poured out') is passive: the psalmist is not emptying himself but being drained. The verse continues: hāyâ libbî kaddônāg nāmēs bĕtôk mēʿāy, 'my heart has become like wax, melted in the midst of my inward parts.' Wax melting in heat loses its form — the heart's structural capacity for emotion and decision collapses. Early Christian reflection saw in these physical descriptions a prophetic anticipation of crucifixion's physiological reality (hypovolemic shock, joint dislocation).
15: yābēš kāḥerpĕśî kōḥî ûlĕšônî mĕdubbāq malqôḥāy (yāḇēš ḵāḥerpĕśî ḵōḥî ûlĕšônî mĕḏubbāq malqôḥāy): 'my strength is dried up like a potsherd; and my tongue cleaves to my jaws.' The simile kāḥerpĕśî ('like a potsherd') — a broken fragment of fired clay, completely dehydrated — captures the state of desperate thirst and physical exhaustion. The phrase ûlĕšônî mĕdubbāq malqôḥāy, 'my tongue sticks to my jaws,' is vivid dehydration — the tongue literally adhering to the mouth from lack of moisture. The closing statement wĕlāʿāpar māwet tišpĕtēnî, 'and you lay me in the dust of death,' makes YHWH the agent of this extremity — a radical theology of divine sovereignty over suffering.
16: kî sĕbābûnî kĕlābîm ʿădat mĕrēʿîm hiqqîpûnî (kî sĕḇāḇûnî ḵĕlāḇîm ʿăḏaṯ mĕrēʿîm hiqqîp̄ûnî): 'for dogs encompass me; a company of evildoers encircles me.' The enemies shift from bulls (Psalms 22:12) to dogs — the unclean scavengers of the ancient world who consumed corpses. The phrase kārû yādāy wĕraglāy, traditionally rendered 'they have pierced my hands and my feet,' is textually disputed: the MT has kāʾărî ('like a lion'), while some Hebrew manuscripts, the LXX, and Syriac read kārû ('they dug/pierced'). The piercing reading became the primary NT exegetical reading (John 19:37; Luke 24:39; Revelation 1:7) for the crucifixion. The Masoretic 'like a lion' makes grammatical sense as an extension of the predator imagery.
17: ʾăsappēr kol-ʿaṣmôtāy hēmmâ yabbîṭû yirʾû-bî (ʾăsappēr ḵol-ʿaṣmôṯāy hēmmâ yabbîṭû yirʾû-ḇî): 'I can count all my bones — they stare and gloat over me.' The ability to count bones (ʾăsappēr kol-ʿaṣmôtāy) describes the emaciated body: skin pulled tight over protruding bones, every rib visible. The enemies observe this display with yabbîṭû ('they stare') and yirʾû ('they look') — their gaze is that of spectators at an execution, unmoved and satisfied. The spectacle of the dying righteous man as public entertainment is the social context of the passion: 'And sitting down they watched him there' (Matthew 27:36).
18: yĕḥallĕqû bĕgādāy lāhem wĕʿal-lĕbûšî yappîlû gôrāl (yĕḥallĕqû ḇĕḡāḏāy lāhem wĕʿal-lĕḇûšî yappîlû ḡôrāl): 'they divide my garments among them, and for my clothing they cast lots.' The verb ḥālaq (piel, 'to divide, apportion') and the lot-casting (yappîlû gôrāl) for the outer garment are among the most literally-fulfilled prophetic details in the Psalter. John 19:23-24 cites this verse explicitly: 'that the Scripture might be fulfilled, they said, Let us not tear it, but cast lots for it.' John's citation names two garments — the divided outer garments and the un-divided tunic — corresponding to the psalm's two-part description. The precision of the fulfillment citation is unusual even by NT standards.
19: wĕʾattâ YHWH ʾal-tirḥāq ʾeyālûtî lĕʿezrātî ḥûšâ (wĕʾattâ YHWH ʾal-tirḥāq ʾeyālûṯî lĕʿezrāṯî ḥûšâ): 'But you, O LORD, be not far off; O my strength, come quickly to my aid!' The waw-adversative wĕʾattâ ('but you') — the same structure as Psalms 22:3 and Psalms 22:9 — marks a repeated pivot from suffering to address to God. The word ʾeyālûtî ('my strength/might' — from ʾayil, 'ram, strong one') is an unusual divine title, used only here in the Psalter. The imperative ḥûšâ ('hurry!') — from ḥûš ('to move quickly') — adds urgency: not merely 'come' but 'come now.'
20: haṣṣîlâ mēḥereb napšî miyyad-keleb yĕḥîdātî (haṣṣîlâ mēḥereḇ napšî miyyaḏ-ḵeleḇ yĕḥîḏāṯî): 'Deliver my soul from the sword, my precious life from the power of the dog.' The hiphil haṣṣîlâ ('deliver, snatch away') is an urgent rescue verb. The three paired enemies — sword (ḥereb), dog (keleb), lion (Psalms 22:21) — represent different modes of violent death. The word yĕḥîdātî ('my only one, my precious/unique life') from yāḥîd ('only, solitary') intensifies the stakes: the psalmist has only one life; its loss cannot be recovered. The LXX reads 'my unique soul' — the soul's uniqueness making its loss irreversible.
21: hôšîʿēnî mippî ʾaryēh ûmiqarnê rēmîm ʿănîtānî (hôšîʿēnî mippî ʾaryēh ûmiqarnê rēmîm ʿănîtānî): 'Save me from the mouth of the lion; from the horns of the wild oxen you have answered me!' The verse is theologically pivotal: the lament section ends and the praise section begins, but the transition is grammatically abrupt. The qal perfect ʿănîtānî ('you have answered me') is unexpected — suddenly the rescue is narrated as accomplished. Whether this represents a sudden assurance experienced in prayer, or a shift to prophetic confidence, the effect is that death's worst threat (Psalms 22:15-18) yields to divine answer. The psalm turns on this verb.
22: ʾăsappĕrâ šimkā lĕʾaḥay bĕtôk qāhāl ʾăhallĕlekā (ʾăsappĕrâ šimkā lĕʾaḥay bĕṯôḵ qāhāl ʾăhallĕleḵā): 'I will tell of your name to my brothers; in the midst of the congregation I will praise you.' The cohortative ʾăsappĕrâ ('I will recount') signals the vow of praise that typically concludes the lament, but here it launches an extended praise section (Psalms 22:22-31). Hebrews 2:12 cites this verse as the voice of Jesus: 'I will tell of your name to my brothers, in the midst of the congregation I will sing your praise' — understanding the risen Christ as the one who declares the Father's name to the church. The congregation (qāhāl) is the community of the redeemed.
23: yirʾê YHWH hallĕlûhû kol-zeraʿ yaʿăqōb kabbĕdûhû (yirʾê YHWH hallĕlûhû ḵol-zeraʿ yaʿăqōḇ kaббĕḏûhû): 'You who fear the LORD, praise him! All you offspring of Jacob, glorify him!' The imperatives — hallĕlûhû ('praise him!'), kabbĕdûhû ('glorify him!'), wĕgûrû mimmennû ('stand in awe of him!') — summon three concentric groups: those who fear YHWH, Jacob's offspring, and Israel. The universal address begins here and expands in Psalms 22:27-31. The ground: kî lōʾ-bāzâ wĕlōʾ šiqqaṣ ʿănût ʿānî wĕlōʾ-histîr pānāyw mimmennû, 'for he has not despised or scorned the suffering of the afflicted one; he has not hidden his face from him' (Psalms 22:24). The praise pivots on the unexpected answer of Psalms 22:21.
24: kî lōʾ-bāzâ wĕlōʾ šiqqaṣ ʿănût ʿānî (kî lōʾ-ḇāzâ wĕlōʾ šiqqaṣ ʿănaṯ ʿānî): 'for he has not despised or abhorred the affliction of the afflicted.' The double negation — lōʾ-bāzâ ('has not despised') and lōʾ šiqqaṣ ('has not treated as detestable/abhorred') — are the strongest possible negatives applied to the failure mode the lament feared. The noun ʿănût ʿānî ('the affliction of the afflicted') encompasses the entire suffering of Psalms 22:6-21. The theological revolution: what appeared as divine abandonment (Psalms 22:1) was actually sustained divine attention — ûbĕšawwĿʿô ʾēlāyw šāmaʿ, 'and when he cried to him, he heard.' The hearing was always happening; the abandonment was perceived, not real.
25: mēʾittĕkā tĕhillātî bĕqāhāl rāb (mēʾittĕḵā tĕhillātî bĕqāhāl rāḇ): 'From you comes my praise in the great assembly.' The phrase mēʾittĕkā ('from you') is theologically significant: even the psalmist's praise is a divine gift, originating in YHWH rather than in the psalmist's autonomous virtue. The plural nĕdārāy ʾăšallēm, 'I will pay my vows' — the vows made in distress (Psalms 22:22) are now fulfilled in the public worship of the qāhāl rāb ('great congregation'). The assembly of worshippers becomes the theater in which the drama of lament-answered-by-rescue is publicly proclaimed.
26: yōʾkĕlû ʿănāwîm wĕyiśbāʿû (yōʾḵĕlû ʿănāwîm wĕyiśbāʿû): 'The afflicted shall eat and be satisfied.' The shared meal — yōʾkĕlû ... wĕyiśbāʿû ('they eat and are satisfied') — is the communal dimension of the rescue: the ʿănāwîm ('afflicted, poor, humble') who share the psalmist's condition share also in the celebration. The phrase yĕhallĕlû YHWH dōrĕšāyw yĕḥî lĕbabkem lāʿad, 'those who seek him shall praise the LORD; may your hearts live forever!' introduces yĕḥî lĕbabkem lāʿad ('may your hearts live forever') — a blessing of perpetual life tied to the community of YHWH-seekers. The meal's eschatological overtones anticipate the messianic banquet (Revelation 19:9).
27: yizkĕrû wĕyāšûbû ʾel-YHWH kol-ʾapsê-ʾāreṣ (yizkĕrû wĕyāšûḇû ʾel-YHWH ḵol-ʾapsê-ʾāreṣ): 'All the ends of the earth shall remember and turn to the LORD.' The scope expands from the congregation (Psalms 22:25-26) to the universe: kol-ʾapsê-ʾāreṣ ('all the ends of the earth') and kol-mišpĕḥôt haggôyim ('all the families of the nations'). The verbs — yizkĕrû ('they shall remember'), yāšûbû ('they shall turn/return'), wĕyištaḥăwû ('they shall bow down') — describe the conversion of the nations as remembering + turning + worship. This sequence mirrors the missionary vision of Psalms 67:2-3 and the NT universal proclamation (Acts 1:8; Revelation 7:9).
28: kî laYHWH hammelûkâ ûmōšēl baggôyim (kî laYHWH hammelûkâ ûmōšēl baggôyim): 'for kingship belongs to the LORD, and he rules over the nations.' The divine kingship declaration laYHWH hammelûkâ is absolute: the definite article on hammelûkâ ('the kingship') asserts that there is only one legitimate sovereign, and it belongs to YHWH. The participle mōšēl ('the one who rules') expresses ongoing governance. This verse is the theological center of Psalms 22:27-31: the universal conversion of nations is grounded in the universality of YHWH's sovereignty — all must turn to him because he already rules them.
29: ʾāklû wĕhištaḥăwû kol-dišnê-ʾereṣ (ʾāḵlû wĕhišṯaḥăwû ḵol-dišnê-ʾereṣ): 'All the prosperous of the earth eat and bow down.' The phrase dišnê-ʾereṣ ('the fat ones of the earth') — the wealthy and powerful — also bow. Worship is not limited to the poor (Psalms 22:26) but extends to the prosperous. The counterpart: kol-yôrĕdê ʿāpār wĕnapšô lōʾ ḥiyyâ, 'before him shall bow all who go down to the dust, even the one who cannot keep his soul alive.' Even the dying, those reduced to dust (returning to Genesis 3:19's ʿāpār), worship. Death itself is not an exception to divine sovereignty.
30: zeraʿ yaʿăbĕdennû yĕsuppār laʾdōnāy laddôr (zeraʿ yaʿăḇoḏennû yĕsuppār laʾḏōnāy laḏḏôr): 'A seed shall serve him; it shall be told of the Lord to the coming generation.' The noun zeraʿ ('seed, offspring') looks forward: the rescue of the psalmist generates a continuing community of servants. The phrase yĕsuppār laʾdōnāy laddôr ('it shall be told of the Lord to the coming generation') describes inter-generational witness — each generation hears and tells. The passive yĕsuppār ('it shall be told') implies that the story propagates on its own power: the report of YHWH's rescue is self-perpetuating good news.
31: yābōʾû wĕyaggîdû ṣidqātô lĕʿam nôlād kî ʿāśâ (yāḇōʾû wĕyaḡḡîdû ṣiḏqāṯô lĕʿam nôlāḏ kî ʿāśâ): 'They shall come and proclaim his righteousness to a people yet to be born, that he has done it.' The final phrase kî ʿāśâ, 'that he has done it' — two Hebrew words, among the most theologically dense in the Psalter — is the summary of the psalm's entire movement: God acted. This bare declaration is the kerygmatic core: from dereliction (Psalms 22:1) to proclamation (Psalms 22:31), the entire psalm is the testimony of one who cried out, was answered, and now tells. The psalm ends not with the psalmist's experience but with the report going forward to ʿam nôlād ('a people yet to be born') — the endless echo of rescue through history.
